name: Milk, human (mature breast milk) (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fresh milk (density ~1.03 g/mL; per 100 g ~= per 97 mL)

components.name: lactose
components.source: USDA FDC 171279 (carbohydrate 6.89 g/100g, essentially all lactose) / NBK235590 Nutrition During Lactation (70 g/L)
components.evidence: DOMINANT carbon/energy source. The FooDB profile listed 'sucrose' for this food, which is wrong: the human-milk disaccharide is lactose (glucose-galactose), 67-78 g/L, ~200 mM.

components.name: human milk oligosaccharides (total HMO)
components.source: Soyyilmaz et al. 2021, Nutrients 13(8):2737 'The Mean of Milk' (mature-milk mean ~11.3 g/L)
components.evidence: Third-largest solid in human milk after lactose and fat. Largely indigestible by the infant but a major fermentable substrate for gut bacteria (Bifidobacterium), so growth-critical for a microbial medium. Built from glucose, galactose, N-acetylglucosamine, fucose, sialic acid.

components.name: 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL)
components.source: Soyyilmaz et al. 2021, Nutrients 13(8):2737 (mature milk ~1.64 g/L)
components.evidence: Most abundant single HMO in secretor mothers; representative fermentable oligosaccharide.

components.name: D-glucose (free)
components.source: Faulkner & Peaker 1982, J Dairy Research 'Metabolic significance of milk glucose' (aqueous-phase free glucose ~2 mM in mature milk)
components.evidence: Small but readily fermentable free monosaccharide; rises from ~0.1 mM (6 h) to steady ~1-2 mM in mature milk.

components.name: D-galactose (free)
components.source: NBK235590 (present as free monosaccharide and HMO building block)
components.evidence: Present at low free concentration; not routinely quantified. Included because galactose is a distinct fermentable sugar for microbes.

components.name: myo-inositol
components.source: Paquette et al. 2023, PNAS 120 doi:10.1073/pnas.2221413120 (mature milk 14.9 +/- 3.1 mg/100 mL, ~93% free myo-inositol)
components.evidence: Abundant free cyclitol, usable carbon source and osmolyte.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Holt 1993 & casein-micelle literature (citrate 2.1-2.8 mM in human milk); Garnsworthy/JDS citrate studies
components.evidence: THE organic acid the FooDB profile omitted. Citrate 2.1-2.8 mM (midpoint ~2.5 mM); part is free/soluble, part complexed with Ca/Mg. A fermentable carbon source and Ca/Mg chelator.

components.name: lactic acid
components.source: Faulkner et al., milk lactate studies (low in fresh aseptic human milk, <1-2 mM)
components.evidence: Low in freshly expressed sterile human milk; rises on microbial souring. Included with null amount as it can be a carbon source once present.

components.name: total protein
components.source: USDA FDC 171279 / NBK235590 (8-9 g/L true protein)
components.evidence: Low-protein food. Whey-dominant (alpha-lactalbumin, lactoferrin, sIgA, lysozyme) with casein; whey:casein shifts ~80:20 early to ~50:50 late lactation. ~20-25% of total N is non-protein N.

components.name: taurine
components.source: Rassin/Wharton and Yamawaki 1998 (PMID 9635064); mature milk ~300 umol/L (~335 nmol/mL)
components.evidence: Abundant FREE amino acid missing from the USDA/FooDB panel (taurine is not protein-bound and not in the USDA amino-acid table). ~300 umol/L (~3.8 mg/100 g).

components.name: L-glutamic acid (glutamate)
components.source: USDA FDC 171279 (total). Free glutamate ~1.25-1.75 mM: van Sadelhoff 2020, Nutrients (PMC7270293)
components.evidence: Most abundant total amino acid; free glutamate ~1.5 mM is >50% of free amino acids and a key microbial N/C source.

components.name: L-glutamine (free)
components.source: van Sadelhoff et al. 2020, Nutrients (PMC7270293) (up to ~0.6 mM in mature milk)
components.evidence: Free glutamine rises ~350% months 1-6; glutamate+glutamine ~70% of total free amino acids. Not resolved in USDA protein-AA panel; added as growth-relevant free N source.

components.name: L-aspartic acid (aspartate)
components.evidence: Total protein-derived + free.

components.name: total fat
components.evidence: ~98% triacylglycerol; major energy source. Individual fatty acids below.

components.name: palmitic acid (16:0)
components.evidence: Dominant saturated FA; largely sn-2 esterified in human milk.

components.name: oleic acid (18:1)
components.evidence: Dominant monounsaturated FA.

components.name: linoleic acid (18:2 n-6)
components.evidence: Main PUFA; essential FA.

components.name: cholesterol
components.evidence: GENUINE constituent of human milk (species-appropriate animal food); NOT noise here even though it would be for a plant food.

components.name: calcium
components.evidence: Partly casein-bound, partly soluble citrate complex.

components.name: chloride
components.evidence: Not in USDA panel; ~12 mmol/L (~42 mg/100g).

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Low free iron; much bound to lactoferrin.

components.name: iodine
components.source: Human-milk iodine literature (~5-15 ug/100 mL, intake-dependent)
components.evidence: Not in USDA panel; retained (current profile lists iodine). Highly maternal-intake dependent.

components.name: cobalt
components.source: present as constituent of vitamin B12
components.evidence: Trace; essentially all as cobalamin. Free cobalt negligible.

components.name: choline
components.evidence: Now quantified (was name-only in prior profile).

components.name: pantothenic acid (B5)
components.evidence: Now quantified (was name-only).

components.name: biotin (B7)
components.evidence: Not in USDA panel; retained (current profile lists biotin), ~4 ug/L.

components.name: vitamin A (RAE)
components.evidence: Mostly retinol/retinyl esters.

components.name: beta-carotene
components.evidence: Provitamin-A carotenoid, low.

components.name: alpha-tocopherol (vitamin E)
components.source: USDA FDC 171279 (SR value; note some studies report higher ~0.3-0.4 mg/100 mL)

components.name: vitamin K (phylloquinone)
components.evidence: Low; clinically notable deficiency risk.

components.name: vitamin D
components.evidence: Low (~4 IU/100 g); maternal-status dependent.

components.name: water
components.evidence: Bulk solvent of the medium.

remove.name: Sucrose C12H22O11
remove.reason: Mislabel/FooDB noise: human milk contains essentially no sucrose. The 6.9 g/100g carbohydrate is LACTOSE (glucose-galactose). Replaced by lactose as the dominant sugar.

remove.name: (R)-Oxypeucedanin
remove.reason: Furanocoumarin from plants (Apiaceae/citrus); biologically implausible in human milk. FooDB predicted/expected noise, not a measured milk constituent.

missing_added: lactose (dominant carbon source, ~202 mM; was wrongly listed as sucrose)
citric acid / citrate (~2.5 mM) - the omitted organic acid
taurine (~0.30 mM free amino acid, absent from USDA/FooDB panel)
free glutamate (~1.5 mM) and free glutamine (~0.6 mM)
chloride, quantified fatty-acid profile, quantified pantothenate/choline/vitamins/minerals
keep_uncovered: 24,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 (genuine trace vitamin-D metabolite of human milk; no BiGG met)
individual HMO structures beyond 2'-FL: LNT, LNnT, 3-FL, 3'-/6'-sialyllactose, LNFP (genuine, no BiGG met)
lactoferrin, alpha-lactalbumin, lysozyme, sIgA (genuine bioactive whey proteins)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 171279 (Milk, human, mature, fluid; SR Legacy NDB 01107) - full macronutrient, amino-acid, fatty-acid, vitamin, mineral panel
Soyyilmaz et al. 2021, Nutrients 13(8):2737 'The Mean of Milk: HMO concentrations throughout lactation' doi:10.3390/nu13082737
Nutrition During Lactation (IOM), NCBI Bookshelf NBK235590 - lactose 70 g/L, protein 8-9 g/L, minerals, taurine
van Sadelhoff et al. 2020, Nutrients (PMC7270293) - free amino acids: glutamate ~1.5 mM, glutamine ~0.6 mM
Faulkner & Peaker 1982, J Dairy Research - milk free glucose ~2 mM
Human-milk citrate literature (Holt 1993; casein-micelle studies) - citrate 2.1-2.8 mM
Paquette et al. 2023, PNAS 120 doi:10.1073/pnas.2221413120 - myo-inositol 14.9 mg/100 mL
Yamawaki et al. 1998 (PMID 9635064) - taurine ~300 umol/L in mature milk
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources for a microbial medium are LACTOSE (~200 mM) plus a large fermentable HMO pool (~11 g/L, uniquely human) and fat (~4.4 g/100 g); citrate (~2.5 mM) is the key organic acid the prior FooDB profile omitted. Protein is low (~1 g/100 g, whey-dominant) but supplemented by a rich free-amino-acid pool (glutamate/glutamine/taurine). The prior profile's 'sucrose' was corrected to lactose, and the plant furanocoumarin (R)-oxypeucedanin removed as FooDB noise.
